1: Job’s response begins with blistering sarcasm that inverts the conventional expressions of gratitude for counsel received. Where conventional wisdom discourse would begin with acknowledgment of the interlocutor’s helpful instruction (as Eliphaz began Job 22:2–4 with rhetorical questions about human-divine benefit), Job’s “how you have helped him who has no power!” turns each expected affirmation into a reproach. In the ancient wisdom tradition, formal courtesy in debate was expected; Job’s direct sarcasm represents a sustained refusal to maintain the conventional courtesies of the wisdom dialogue genre.
2: “How you have helped him who has no power! How you have saved the arm that has no strength!” — the ironic structure of vv2–4 is built on the same social-assistance vocabulary that Job’s own noble past (Job 29) will use. In Job 29:12–16, Job describes delivering the poor, saving the fatherless, strengthening the blind and lame — genuine acts of social rescue. Bildad’s speech offered Job none of this actual assistance; it offered only theoretical theology. Job’s sarcasm calls out the gap between the friends’ confident theological discourse and the total absence of practical or empathetic engagement with his actual suffering.
3: “How you have counseled him who has no wisdom!” — the wisdom counsel that Bildad offered is dismissed as non-counsel: what wisdom does it give to tell a suffering man that humans are maggots? In the ancient wisdom tradition, genuine counsel (’etsah) was measured by its practical usefulness to the person in need. The wisdom literature repeatedly distinguishes between counsel that actually helps (Proverbs 20:18: “plans are established by counsel”) and empty speech. Job’s question “and how have you declared sound wisdom abundantly?” (toushiyah, practical wisdom/competence) sarcastically frames Bildad’s abstract theology as a parody of the practical wisdom the situation required.
4: “With whose help have you uttered words, and whose breath has come out from you?” — this verse is a direct challenge to Bildad’s (and perhaps Eliphaz’s) claim to speak with divine authority or inspiration. Eliphaz explicitly claimed divine revelation in Job 4:12–16 (a spirit passing, a whispering voice, a revelation in the night). Bildad has made no such explicit claim, but the friends speak with the authority of the received wisdom tradition. Job challenges the very source of their authority: if their words do not come from genuine encounter with divine reality, who is the breath behind their speech? The question anticipates the divine speeches (Job 38–41) where YHWH will speak from the whirlwind — genuine divine breath.
5: Job’s counter-hymn begins not with the heavens but with the underworld: “the Rephaim tremble below the waters, those who dwell in them” (harefa’im yeholalu mitahat mayim veshokheneyhem). The Rephaim (refa’im) appear in the OT as both: (1) the shades/ghosts of the dead in Sheol (Psalms 88:10; Proverbs 2:18; Job 9:18; Isaiah 14:9; Job 26:14,19); and (2) the giant pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan (Genesis 14:5; Job 15:20; Deuteronomy 2:10–11; Job 3:11). In Ugaritic texts, the Rpum (cognate of Rephaim) were the noble dead warriors who could be summoned for divine feasts. Job’s hymn begins where Bildad ended: if even the cosmic purity of the stars is impure before God (Job 25:5), how much more are the dead in their underworld domain subject to divine sovereignty.
6: “Sheol is naked before God, and Abaddon has no covering” — ’Avaddon appears here as a synonym for Sheol (also Job 28:22; Job 31:12; Psalms 88:11; Proverbs 15:11; Job 27:20). The etymology (aved, destruction/perishing) suggests a realm of destruction; in Revelation 9:11, Abaddon becomes the angel-king of the bottomless pit (“the angel of the abyss, whose name in Hebrew is Abaddon”). The claim that Sheol and Abaddon are arummim (naked/bare, with no covering) before YHWH contrasts sharply with the Mesopotamian underworld theology, where the realm of Irkalla was a semi-autonomous domain under the rule of Nergal and Ereshkigal — not subordinate to the sovereignty of the sky-god. For Job (and for OT theology generally), there is no domain beyond YHWH’s sight.
7: “He stretches out the north (tsafon) over the void, and hangs the earth over nothing” (toleh eretz al-belima) — this is one of the most theologically striking cosmological statements in the OT. The tsafon (north/Zaphon) was also the name of Baal’s sacred mountain in Ugaritic theology (Mount Zaphon = Jebel al-Aqra in northern Syria), the divine mountain of the assembly. Job’s use of tsafon may carry both senses: the literal north and the mythological divine mountain. The phrase toleh eretz al-belima (hanging the earth on nothingness/without support) stands in tension with other OT cosmological images of the earth resting on pillars (Psalms 75:3; 1 Samuel 2:8) or upon the deep (Psalms 24:2). Mesopotamian cosmology described the earth as resting on the Apsu (primordial fresh water beneath the earth). Job’s formulation is unique in the ancient world in its claim that the earth has no physical support.
8: “He binds up the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud is not split open under them” — the image of clouds as divine water-containers reflects the ancient understanding of clouds as the mechanism of rain delivery. In Ugaritic mythology, Baal as the storm-god was the “rider of the clouds” (rkb ’rpt); his control of rain and clouds was the basis of his agricultural importance. YHWH appropriates this imagery: Psalms 104:3 (“he makes the clouds his chariot”); Nahum 1:3 (“his way is in whirlwind and storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet”). The marvel is that clouds hold quantities of water without rupturing — an observation about the physical world that points to divine engineering.
9: “He covers the face of the full moon and spreads over it his cloud” — the exact meaning of kiseh (full moon / throne / covering) in this verse is debated. If “full moon,” the verse describes God’s control over lunar visibility through cloud cover. If “throne,” the verse describes God spreading cloud as a veil over his heavenly throne. In either reading, the divine sovereignty over the upper cosmic realm is the point. The moon (Sin) in Mesopotamian theology was a major deity; for Job, the moon is simply an object under YHWH’s sovereign management.
10: “He has inscribed a circle on the face of the waters at the boundary between light and darkness” — the image of YHWH drawing a circular boundary (hog, inscribed limit) on the surface of the cosmic ocean reflects the ancient cosmological picture of the habitable earth as a disc encircled by the primordial ocean. Proverbs 8:27 uses the identical imagery: “when he established the heavens, I was there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.” The boundary between light and darkness as a divinely inscribed limit reflects the first creative act of Genesis 1:3–4 (God separating light from darkness); here the separation is expressed in terms of the horizontal boundary inscribed on the cosmic waters rather than the temporal separation of day from night.
11: “The pillars of heaven tremble and are astounded at his rebuke” — the pillars of heaven (amudei shamayim) are the cosmological columns supporting the vault of heaven in the ancient ANE worldview. Psalms 75:3: “when the earth totters and all its inhabitants, it is I who keep steady its pillars.” 1 Samuel 2:8: “the pillars of the earth are YHWH’s.” In Phoenician/Canaanite cosmology, the divine mountains at the edges of the world served as heaven’s supports; in Egyptian cosmology, four pillars at the corners of the sky held up the vault. Job’s reference to these pillars trembling at divine rebuke demonstrates YHWH’s power over the very structural supports of the cosmos.
12: “By his power he stilled the sea; by his understanding he struck down Rahab” — Rahab (Rahav) is the primordial sea-chaos monster, parallel to Leviathan, representing the waters of disorder that God defeated in the creation conflict. Rahab appears in Psalms 89:10 (“you crushed Rahab like a carcass; you scattered your enemies with your mighty arm”); Isaiah 51:9 (“was it not you who cut Rahab in pieces, who pierced the dragon?”); Psalms 87:4 (Rahab = Egypt). In Ugaritic mythology, Baal’s victory over Yam (the sea-god) established divine order over chaos; the OT adapts this combat mythology to YHWH’s creation-act. Job’s hymn places the primordial sea-combat in the larger context of YHWH’s cosmic sovereignty over all domains.
13: “By his breath the skies were cleared; his hand pierced the fleeing serpent” (bariah nahash) — the “fleeing serpent” formula appears nearly verbatim in Isaiah 27:1: “in that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing (bariah) serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea.” The nahash bariah is the cosmic chaos-serpent of ANE mythology, equivalent to the Ugaritic Lotan (the “twisting serpent” btn ’qltn) whom Baal defeated in the Ugaritic Baal cycle. Job uses this standard mythology to assert YHWH’s victory over the chaos-powers — not Baal’s victory, but YHWH’s. The breath (nishmo) of YHWH that clears the skies also points back to Genesis 2:7 (the divine breath that animated humanity).
14: “These are the outer fringes of his ways (qetsot derakhav); how small a whisper do we hear of him! Who could understand the thunder of his power?” — this concluding verse is one of the most profound epistemological statements in the book. All the cosmic wonders just catalogued — the conquered Rephaim, the naked Sheol, the suspended earth, the cloud-bound water, the circular horizon, the trembling pillars, the defeated Rahab, the pierced serpent — these are only the outer edge, the margins, the fringe (qetsot) of YHWH’s actual ways. The Ludlul bel nemeqi similarly concludes with a statement of divine incomprehensibility: “who can know the will of the gods in heaven? Where has confounded mortal ever learned what a god’s plan is?” Job’s hymn surpasses Bildad’s Job 25 speech in comprehensiveness, grandeur, and humility — demonstrating that Job understands divine majesty better than his accusers.
